Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Policy Name/Number: Request for Information – Accelerating the Adoption and Use of Artificial Intelligence as Part of Clinical Care
Summary: HHS issued an RFI, led by the Office of the Deputy Secretary and ASTP/ONC, seeking public input on how the Department can accelerate adoption and use of AI as part of clinical care. The notice asks stakeholders to describe promising AI use cases, barriers to adoption, and concrete actions HHS should take across regulation, reimbursement, health IT policy, and research & development to enable safe, effective, and trustworthy AI in care delivery.
Healthcare Implications: The RFI signals a coordinated, cross-HHS effort to reshape the national policy environment for clinical AI. Depending on responses, it could lead to future changes in FDA oversight of AI-enabled devices, CMS payment rules for AI-supported services, ONC health IT certification criteria, and NIH and agency funding priorities. Health systems, clinicians, patients, vendors, and payers are explicitly invited to help define guardrails, incentives, and infrastructure to support high-quality, equitable, and deflationary AI-enabled care.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M-26-04 – Increasing Public Trust in Artificial Intelligence Through Unbiased AI Principles
Summary: OMB memo implementing “Unbiased AI Principles” for large language models and other AI systems procured by federal agencies, requiring documentation, disclosures, and mechanisms to address bias, inaccuracies, and misuse.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to LLMs and other AI systems used in federal health programs, benefits portals, and clinical or administrative tools, pushing agencies and vendors to demonstrate truthfulness, objectivity, and bias mitigation in models that may inform patient communication, triage, and benefits decisions, thereby supporting more equitable and trustworthy health-AI deployments.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Equity & Bias; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14365 – Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Establishes a national policy framework for artificial intelligence that favors a minimally burdensome federal approach, directs the creation of a DOJ AI Litigation Task Force to challenge state and local AI laws that conflict with federal policy, instructs agencies to identify and preempt inconsistent state requirements, and links certain federal funding and regulatory actions to state compliance with the national AI framework.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects the landscape for state AI-in-healthcare laws and regulations by inviting federal challenges to stricter state rules on algorithmic discrimination, transparency, and safety; this could weaken or chill state-level protections around clinical AI, insurance and utilization-review algorithms, and public-benefits decision systems, while centralizing more authority for AI governance within federal agencies that oversee health and human services.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Equity & Bias; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14355 – Unlocking Cures for Pediatric Cancer with Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directs the Department of Health and Human Services, the National Institutes of Health, and other agencies to harness AI to accelerate pediatric cancer research and care. It expands federal support for the Childhood Cancer Data Initiative and encourages secure use of electronic health record and claims data to improve diagnostics, treatment discovery, and prevention.
Healthcare Implications: Providers and researchers can expect expanded funding opportunities, data access, and pilots for AI in oncology. Organizations should align their data governance with privacy and transparency requirements and strengthen validation and safety monitoring for pediatric AI tools.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14319 – Preventing Woke AI in the Federal Government
Summary: Directs that AI systems used or procured by the federal government be "ideologically neutral" and not promote specified concepts, and tasks agencies and the Office of Management and Budget with aligning acquisition, testing, and reporting to those principles. Establishes definitions, assigns agency duties, and requires attestations/assurances from vendors supplying AI systems to the government.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to HHS, VA, and other health agencies that deploy or procure AI (e.g., chatbots, decision support, triage). May constrain equity-focused AI features (e.g., bias-mitigation or inclusive design) in federal systems and require vendor attestations on neutrality; contracts and 'Authorization to Operate' packages will need updated compliance language.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M-25-21 – Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust
Summary: Government-wide OMB memo that rescinds M-24-10 and establishes new requirements for how federal agencies identify, govern, and expand their use of AI, including inventories of AI use cases, risk assessment processes, governance structures, and public reporting.
Healthcare Implications: Requires HHS, CMS, NIH, CDC, VA, and other health-related agencies to maintain AI use inventories, implement AI risk management and governance processes, and safeguard civil rights, privacy, and safety when using AI in clinical programs, public health surveillance, and health benefits administration.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M-25-22 – Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government
Summary: Companion OMB memo that rescinds M-24-18 and sets standards for federal agencies to acquire AI responsibly, emphasizing competition in the AI marketplace, lifecycle risk management, performance monitoring, and safeguards for privacy and civil rights in AI contracts.
Healthcare Implications: Shapes how federal health agencies and programs procure AI-enabled clinical, analytic, and administrative tools by requiring acquisition processes that incorporate AI risk assessment, privacy and civil-rights protections, performance monitoring, and vendor accountability into contracts for health-related AI systems.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14179 – Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Executive order that declares it U.S. policy to sustain and enhance American leadership in artificial intelligence, revokes prior AI directives viewed as barriers to innovation (including the Biden Administration’s EO 14110), and directs agencies and OMB to review, suspend, or revise AI-related regulations, guidance, and procurement rules that are considered unnecessarily restrictive.
Healthcare Implications: Resets the federal AI governance baseline for HHS, CMS, FDA, VA, and other health-related agencies by shifting emphasis from precautionary safety obligations toward innovation, which may lead to revision or rollback of earlier AI risk management requirements that health programs and contractors had begun to implement, including how “rights-impacting” and “safety-impacting” AI in healthcare are classified and overseen.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Policy Name/Number: Strategic Plan for the Use of Artificial Intelligence in Health, Human Services, and Public Health
Summary: HHS-wide AI strategic plan outlining goals and actions for using AI across healthcare, human services, and public health, including governance, workforce development, research and innovation, data infrastructure, and program operations.
Healthcare Implications: Signals how HHS will oversee, fund, and deploy AI in clinical care, coverage and payment decision-making, public health surveillance, and social service programs, emphasizing safety, equity, transparency, and regulatory clarity for AI tools used in health and human services.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M-24-18 – Advancing the Responsible Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government
Summary: Government-wide OMB guidance that sets requirements and best practices for federal acquisition of AI systems and services, including planning, market research, evaluation criteria, contract terms, and post-award governance, with special attention to AI systems that are rights-impacting or safety-impacting and to alignment with existing AI use guidance such as M-24-10.
Healthcare Implications: For HHS, CMS, CDC, NIH, VA, DoD health, and other health-related programs, this memorandum shapes how clinical decision support tools, claims and fraud analytics, public health surveillance systems, and other AI-enabled technologies are procured, pushing agencies to embed risk management, evaluation, transparency, and performance safeguards into contracts in ways that will cascade to health IT vendors and their compliance obligations.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Policy Name/Number: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance (Section 504)
Summary: Finalizes the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services update to Section 504 regulations, strengthening requirements for effective communication, medical treatment non‑discrimination, web and mobile accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA), and protections in value‑assessment methods. Clarifies responsibilities for recipients of HHS funding and coordinates with related civil‑rights frameworks.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare providers, plans, and grantees must ensure accessibility across digital tools, clinical communications, and facility operations. AI‑mediated services (bots, decision support) must not result in disability discrimination and should follow accessibility standards and human‑in‑the‑loop practices aligned with Section 504 and related §1557 protections.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Issuing Body: Department of Health and Human Services
Policy Name/Number: Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability in Programs or Activities Receiving Federal Financial Assistance
Summary: Finalizes disability nondiscrimination requirements for programs receiving federal financial assistance, including effective communication and web/mobile accessibility.
Healthcare Implications: Covered health entities must ensure accessibility; the Office of Civil Rights notes risks from automated decision‑making, complementing §1557 protections.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: OMB Memorandum M‑24‑10 – Advancing Governance, Innovation, and Risk Management for Agency Use of AI
Summary: Sets government‑wide AI governance requirements, including designating Chief AI Officers, maintaining AI use inventories, implementing risk management practices for rights- and safety‑impacting AI, and establishing procurement and testing standards. This Biden-era policy has been superseded by more recent OMB memorandums.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, the Department of Veteran Affairs, the Department of Defense health programs, and contractors integrating AI into public‑facing or rights‑impacting services. Drives inventories, impact assessments, and assurance processes that affect clinical decision support, benefits eligibility automation, and health IT procurements.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14120 – Advancing Women's Health Research and Innovation
Summary: Launches a federal initiative to accelerate women’s health research and translation, coordinating across agencies and prioritizing funding, data, and innovation activities. Calls for closing evidence gaps and mobilizing cross‑sector collaboration.
Healthcare Implications: Encourages AI‑enabled research and diagnostic development specific to women’s health (e.g., imaging, cardiology, maternal outcomes). Signals funding and data priorities relevant to health systems, researchers, and AI developers.
Keyword Tags: Privacy & Data; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14117 – Preventing Access to Americans’ Bulk Sensitive Personal Data and U.S. Government‑Related Data by Countries of Concern
Summary: Addresses national‑security risks from transfers of bulk sensitive personal data (including health, genomic, biometric, precise geolocation) to countries of concern. Directs the Department of Justice and other agencies to propose regulations restricting certain transactions and establishing safeguards.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems, data brokers, and app developers handling health/genomic data must evaluate cross‑border data flows and third‑party relationships. AI training and analytics that use sensitive datasets will need vendor due diligence and technical/contractual controls to avoid prohibited transfers.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14110 – Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directed a whole‑of‑government approach to AI safety, security, and equity, including safety test reporting for powerful models, standards development at the National Institute of Standards and Technology, infrastructure security, and civil‑rights protections. Provided agency tasks on healthcare, workforce, and R&D.
Healthcare Implications: Spurred the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services and other agencies to evaluate risks in clinical algorithms, bias in health programs, and AI safety testing. Note: EO 14110 was revoked by EO 14179 (Jan. 23, 2025); agencies were instructed to review and modify actions taken under 14110. Some derivative guidance (e.g., OMB M‑24‑10) remains in effect unless separately rescinded.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: National Artificial Intelligence Research and Development Strategic Plan – 2023 Update
Summary: Sets federal priorities across nine strategic areas for AI research, including trustworthy AI, safety, privacy‑preserving techniques, human‑AI collaboration, and evaluation; calls for robust testbeds, datasets, and workforce development. Guides multi‑agency R&D coordination and investment.
Healthcare Implications: Identifies research thrusts directly relevant to health AI – privacy‑preserving learning, robustness, evaluation benchmarks, and human‑centered design. Shapes the National Institute of Health's, the National Science Foundation's, and agency grantmaking that providers and developers can leverage for clinical AI.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 14091 – Further Advancing Racial Equity and Support for Underserved Communities Through the Federal Government
Summary: Builds on EO 13985 to embed equity across federal programs, including establishing agency equity teams, improving data collection, and addressing discrimination risks in automated technology. Sets annual agency action plans and accountability structures.
Healthcare Implications: Reinforces civil‑rights expectations for health programs and federal contractors, including attention to algorithmic bias in benefits and service delivery. Supports equitable access initiatives and data practices relevant to AI tools used by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services and health grantees.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Public Law 117-207 (SB2551) – Artificial Intelligence Training for the Acquisition Workforce Act
Summary: Directs the Office of Management and Budget to establish AI training for the federal acquisition workforce, covering AI benefits, risks, ethics, and procurement best practices; requires agency participation and updates.
Healthcare Implications: Elevates due diligence for AI-enabled health IT procurements across federal health agencies (HHS, VA), impacting vendor requirements for explainability, bias mitigation, and data rights.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights
Summary: Outlines five principles for safe, effective, and rights‑preserving automated systems: safe and effective systems; algorithmic discrimination protections; data privacy; notice and explanation; and human alternatives/consideration/fallback. Accompanied by a technical companion and examples to guide implementation.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a policy baseline for health organizations building/using AI: emphasize safety/effectiveness validation, discrimination testing, privacy by design for sensitive health data, clear patient/provider notices, and human‑in‑the‑loop options for consequential decisions.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: Advancing Biotechnology and Biomanufacturing Innovation for a Sustainable, Safe, and Secure American Bioeconomy
Summary: Directs a whole‑of‑government strategy to advance biotechnology and biomanufacturing via R&D, domestic capacity, biosafety/biosecurity, and data infrastructure; requires agency plans and reports.
Healthcare Implications: Connects AI with biological data analysis, forecasting, and design; encourages AI‑enabled discovery in genomics, therapeutics, and bioproduction.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: Executive Order 13859 – Maintaining American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Launches the American AI Initiative, prioritizing R&D, data access, workforce development, and international engagement to sustain U.S. AI leadership. Directs agencies to prioritize AI in budgets and policy coordination.
Healthcare Implications: Catalyzed agency AI strategies and research initiatives relevant to health AI (NIH, FDA, HHS). Framed open data and R&D priorities that supported clinical AI research and the health‑AI workforce.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors
